items.foco: Definition: refractive error due to excess dioptric power
items.title: Definition of myopia
items.contentHtml:
Definition

  • Myopia: refractive error due to excess dioptric power of the ocular optical system, which causes parallel light rays to converge in front of the retina in an eye at accommodative rest.
  • It is quantified in negative diopters (e.g. -3.00 D). The remote point is at a finite distance from the eye.
  • Differentiate from pseudomyopia: reversible accommodative spasm that simulates myopia, resolving with cycloplegia.

items.foco: Classification: axial vs refractive myopia (curvature or index)
items.title: Pathophysiological classification
items.contentHtml:
Classification: axial vs refractive myopia

  • Axial myopia: the most common (>90% of cases). Caused by lengthening of the anteroposterior axis of the eyeball >24 mm.
  • Refractive myopia: due to excess corneal curvature (e.g. keratoconus) or the lens (e.g. nuclear cataract), or due to an increase in the refractive index of the lens (e.g. diabetes).
  • The combination is possible. Etiological classification guides surgical management (e.g. LASIK not indicated in keratoconus).

items.foco: Etiology: elongation of the anteroposterior axis of the eyeball
items.title: Etiology and risk factors
items.contentHtml:
Etiology

  • Main cause: lengthening of the anteroposterior axis of the eyeball due to excessive scleral growth, mediated by genetic and environmental factors.
  • Key environmental factor: prolonged close work (reading, screens) that stimulates the local release of insufficient retinal dopamine.
  • Polygenic inheritance: risk 3-4 times higher if both Parents are short-sighted.

items.foco: Symptoms: blurred distance vision, squinting, headache
items.title: Clinical symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Symptoms

  • Blurred distance vision that improves by squinting the eyelids (pinhole effect).
  • Frontal headache and visual fatigue (asthenopia) due to accommodative effort or overcorrection.
  • In children: decreased school performance, excessive proximity to the blackboard or book.

items.foco: Diagnosis: retinoscopy and autorefraction under cycloplegia
items.title: Confirmatory diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis

  • Retinoscopy: objective method that detects the neutral point with divergent lenses; the “against” movement indicates myopia.
  • Autorefraction under cycloplegia (e.g. cyclopentolate 1% or tropicamide 1%) is the gold standard to rule out pseudomyopia and obtain the real value.
  • uncorrected visual acuity is measured with optotypes; best corrected visual acuity should be 20/20 or better.

items.foco: Optical treatment: divergent lenses (concave, negative sign)
items.title: Optical treatment
items.contentHtml:
Optical treatment

  • Correction with divergent lenses (concave, negative sign): they move the focal point back to the retina.
  • In children, complete correction is prescribed under cycloplegia to avoid amblyopia.
  • Contact lenses: soft, rigid gas permeable or orthokeratology (night molding lenses).

items.foco: Surgical correction: LASIK, PRK, LASEK (refractive surgery)
items.title: Refractive surgery
items.contentHtml:
Surgical correction

  • LASIK: creation of a corneal flap with microkeratome or femtosecond, excimer laser ablation. Indicated in stable myopia ≤ -8.00 D.
  • PRK: direct ablation of the corneal epithelium; higher risk of postoperative pain and haze, but useful in thin corneas.
  • LASEK: epithelium-sparing variant; Less discomfort than PRK. Contraindications: keratoconus, insufficient corneal thickness (<480 microns).

items.title: Retinal detachment
items.contentHtml:
Main complication

  • Retinal detachment (increased risk): axial elongation produces peripheral retinal thinning, honeycomb degeneration and retinal tears.
  • The risk is 3-6 times higher in myopes >-6.00 D compared to emmetropes.
  • Symptoms of alarm: myodesopsias (floaters), photopsias, curtain vision. Ophthalmological emergency.

items.foco: Association: open angle glaucoma and high myopia
items.title: Pathological associations
items.contentHtml:
Association: open angle glaucoma

  • Open angle glaucoma: greater prevalence in high myopia (OR 2-3). Axial elongation alters the biomechanics of the cribriform plate.
  • Magna myopia (>-6.00 D) is associated with posterior staphyloma, choroidal neovascularization and myopic maculopathy.
  • Screening: annual fundus examination and measurement of intraocular pressure in all patients with myopia magna.

items.foco: High myopia: >6 diopters, increased risk of retinal pathology
items.title: High myopia and retinal pathology
items.contentHtml:
Great myopia

  • Definition: great myopia >6 diopters or axial length >26.5 mm.
  • Increased risk of retinal pathology: retinal detachment, macular hole, choroidal neovascularization ( Fuchs).
  • Myopic maculopathy is a cause of irreversible visual loss in young adults; It is classified into grades according to chorioretinal atrophy.

items.foco: Prevention: natural light and time outdoors in children
items.title: Prevention in children
items.contentHtml:
Prevention

  • Exposure to natural light (at least 2 hours/day outdoors) reduces the incidence of myopia in children, possibly due to stimulation of retinal dopamine.
  • Outdoor time in children is the preventive intervention with the most evidence; reduces the risk of progression by 30-50%.
  • Avoid prolonged fence work without breaks; the 20-20-20 rule (every 20 minutes look 20 feet away for 20 seconds).

items.foco: Screening: refractive evaluation in school age and adolescents
items.title: Population screening
items.contentHtml:
Screening

  • Refractive evaluation in school age and adolescents: visual screening is recommended in children aged 3-5 years (visual acuity and retinoscopy) and every 1-2 years during school age.
  • Instruments: portable autorefractometers and optotypes; retinoscopy is the reference method.
  • Early detection prevents amblyopia and allows interventions such as topical atropine (0.01%) to stop progression in evolutionary myopia.
